experiment.hypothesis: EIP-8141's frame composition model (DEFAULT/VERIFY/SENDER modes, APPROVE opcode, atomic batching, dual signature schemes) can be modeled as a Solidity router on EVVM with new EVM opcodes mocked as functions/events. This is the foundation experiment — three sibling sub-experiments build on this router for canonical paymaster, default code, and P256 signatures respectively.

requires.notes: Fee market — in every recent Anvil hardfork.
Typed tx envelope — this EIP adds type 0x06, but we don't model the tx-type byte in this Solidity-layer experiment.
Contract-cannot-be-sender — interestingly, EIP-8141 explicitly disables this for frame txs. Documented as a deviation, not modeled here.
Blob tx fee fields are passed through, not interpreted by FrameRouter.
Calldata cost change — affects the EIP's per-frame cost rationale but not the router's contract-layer behavior.
EOA-set-code delegation. Mocked as a small registry contract that FrameRouter consults before treating a sender as EOA-with-default-code.

mocks.name: APPROVE-as-function (FrameRouter.approve)
mocks.reason: Cannot add EVM opcodes locally. VERIFY frames call FrameRouter.approve(scope) — the router updates transient storage (payer, senderApproved) and emits Approved(scope).
mocks.limitation: Loses the opcode's frame-exit-on-success behavior. In the real EIP, APPROVE both terminates the frame AND updates approval state in one step; our mock requires the calling contract to return after invoking approve(). Reviewers should treat this as an integration test, not a faithful gas/atomicity model.

mocks.name: TXPARAM/FRAMEDATA*/FRAMEPARAM/SIGPARAM-as-functions
mocks.reason: Cannot add EVM opcodes. Exposed as view functions on FrameRouter; VERIFY/SENDER frames call back into the router to read tx/frame/sig context.
mocks.limitation: View-function gas cost replaces opcode-immediate cost (the real opcodes are 2-3 gas; our calls cost ~700 gas per access). The 'undefined param values cause exceptional halt' behavior is modeled as require() reverts.

mocks.name: MockExpiryVerifier
mocks.stubs: Canonical expiry verifier system contract at 0x...8141
mocks.reason: EIP §'Expiry Verifier Frame' specifies a 26-byte runtime bytecode that reverts unless block.timestamp <= expiry_timestamp. We provide a Solidity equivalent that's bit-equivalent in behavior; for production fidelity, a deploy script would vm.etch the canonical bytes at 0x...8141.
mocks.limitation: Deployed at a regular address in this experiment, not the canonical address. The behavior is bit-equivalent (same revert/stop semantics).

mocks.name: MockP256Verifier
mocks.reason: DaimoSoftware/p256-verifier ships a battle-tested pure-Solidity P256VERIFY implementation. We wrap it behind FrameRouter's IPSignatureVerifier interface. When EIP-7951 (secp256r1 precompile) ships, swap the vendor for the precompile call.
mocks.limitation: Pure-Solidity P256 costs ~340k gas per verify, vs. ~3-7k for the precompile. The functional behavior is correct; the gas profile is not. Adversarial scenarios about gas exhaustion would not be representative.

contracts.why: Frame transactions are a service-level abstraction (the validate-pay-execute pipeline is composable in any contract that processes the frame array). Building this as a Shape B new service — rather than modifying Core — keeps EVVM's existing nonce/balance machinery untouched and lets FrameRouter compose with the existing dual-signature pattern (the per-frame VERIFY pattern is conceptually identical to EVVM's action+pay split).

The canonical expiry verifier in EIP §'Expiry Verifier Frame' is a tiny system contract at 0x...8141 with a fixed bytecode. We model it as a Solidity contract with bit-equivalent behavior so the FrameRouter's expiry handling can be exercised end-to-end without a vm.etch dance during local testing.

Pluggable P256 verifier. Wraps Daimo's audited Solidity P256VERIFY so FrameRouter can validate P256 signatures (scheme 0x1) without modifying the canonical EVM. Drop-in replacement when EIP-7951's precompile ships.
